sp-sec10-u1-L1-Q1.1: Sputnik 1 was indeed the first artificial satellite, launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957.

sp-sec10-u1-L1-Q2.0: The United States launched Explorer 1 in 1958, several months after the Soviet Union's Sputnik 1.
sp-sec10-u1-L1-Q2.2: The United Kingdom did not have an independent orbital launch capability during the early Space Race.
sp-sec10-u1-L1-Q2.3: While Germany developed V-2 rockets in WWII, it was the Soviet Union that launched the first satellite.

sp-sec10-u1-L1-Q4.0: 1955 is too early; the technology to reach orbit was not ready until late 1957.
sp-sec10-u1-L1-Q4.1: 1961 was the year of the first human spaceflight, not Sputnik's launch.
sp-sec10-u1-L1-Q4.3: 1963 is six years too late; Sputnik launched in October 1957.

sp-sec10-u1-L1-Q5.0: Sputnik 1 carried only a radio transmitter; it had no scientific instruments beyond its beacon signal.

sp-sec10-u1-L2-Q1.0: Explorer 1 launched on January 31, 1958, nearly four months after Sputnik 1 in October 1957.

sp-sec10-u1-L2-Q2.0: Water on the Moon was not confirmed until decades later by orbital missions and the LCROSS impact.
sp-sec10-u1-L2-Q2.2: Near-Earth asteroid detection came much later with dedicated survey programs, not Explorer 1.
sp-sec10-u1-L2-Q2.3: Radio signals from Jupiter were detected by ground-based radio telescopes, not by Explorer 1.

sp-sec10-u1-L2-Q5.0: Wernher von Braun designed the Juno rocket that launched Explorer 1, but James Van Allen built the instruments.
sp-sec10-u1-L2-Q5.1: Robert Goddard pioneered liquid-fuel rocketry but died in 1945, before Explorer 1.
sp-sec10-u1-L2-Q5.3: Sergei Korolev was the Soviet chief rocket designer who led the Sputnik program, not Explorer 1.

sp-sec10-u1-L2-Q6.1: This is true because Earth's magnetic field captures energetic charged particles from the solar wind, forming the Van Allen belts.

sp-sec10-u1-L3-Q1.1: Yuri Gagarin orbited Earth on April 12, 1961 aboard Vostok 1, making him the first human in space.

sp-sec10-u1-L3-Q2.0: 15 minutes was approximately the duration of Alan Shepard's suborbital flight, not Gagarin's orbital mission.
sp-sec10-u1-L3-Q2.2: 5 hours is too long; Gagarin completed one orbit in 108 minutes and returned immediately.
sp-sec10-u1-L3-Q2.3: 24 hours would be a full day in orbit; Gagarin's mission lasted only one orbit at 108 minutes.

sp-sec10-u1-L3-Q3.0: The spacecraft was relatively simple; the concern was about human cognitive function in weightlessness.
sp-sec10-u1-L3-Q3.1: Gagarin was a trained military pilot; the issue was uncertainty about human performance in space.
sp-sec10-u1-L3-Q3.3: Manual controls existed as backup; ground control was used because of concerns about spaceflight's effects on the human body.

sp-sec10-u1-L3-Q5.0: Gagarin ejected from the capsule at about 7 km altitude and parachuted down separately.

sp-sec10-u2-L1-Q1.1: President Kennedy delivered his famous Moon speech in 1961, setting the deadline for the end of the decade.

sp-sec10-u2-L1-Q2.0: No one had proven reaching the Moon was easy; it was chosen precisely because it was difficult.
sp-sec10-u2-L1-Q2.2: Lunar mining was not a motivation in the 1960s; the goal was Cold War technological supremacy.
sp-sec10-u2-L1-Q2.3: NASA was actually given the Moon goal by the president; it was not a simple mission request.

sp-sec10-u2-L1-Q4.0: 5,000 is far too few; the Apollo program was one of the largest engineering efforts in history.
sp-sec10-u2-L1-Q4.1: 50,000 underestimates the massive workforce; about 400,000 people contributed across government and industry.
sp-sec10-u2-L1-Q4.3: 2 million overstates the number; approximately 400,000 people worked on Apollo at its peak.

sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q1.1: Neil Armstrong was indeed the first person on the Moon, stepping out of the Lunar Module Eagle on July 20, 1969.

sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q2.0: Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon's surface with Armstrong; Michael Collins remained in lunar orbit.
sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q2.2: John Glenn was the first American to orbit Earth in 1962 and was not part of Apollo 11.
sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q2.3: Pete Conrad commanded Apollo 12, the second Moon landing, not Apollo 11.

sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q3.1: Armstrong took manual control out of necessity, not personal glory, because the landing site was dangerous.
sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q3.2: Mission control did not order the switch; Armstrong made the decision based on what he saw through the window.
sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q3.3: The landing gear functioned normally; the issue was the computer guiding the lander toward a boulder field.

sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q6.1: Apollo 11 brought back approximately 21 kilograms of lunar samples, which were extensively studied on Earth.

sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q7.0: 1 million vastly underestimates the global audience; the Moon landing was the most-watched broadcast in history at that time.
sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q7.1: 50 million is too low; an estimated 600 million people worldwide watched the live broadcast.
sp-sec10-u2-L2-Q7.3: 3 billion exceeds the world population at the time (about 3.6 billion), with only 600 million having TV access for the event.

sp-sec10-u2-L3-Q1.0: Apollo 13 never landed on the Moon; the crew used the lunar module as a lifeboat to return safely to Earth.

sp-sec10-u2-L3-Q2.0: No meteor struck Apollo 13; an internal oxygen tank explosion caused the emergency.
sp-sec10-u2-L3-Q2.2: The heat shield was intact; the crisis was caused by an oxygen tank rupture in the service module.
sp-sec10-u2-L3-Q2.3: There was no fuel leak; an electrical short ignited insulation in an oxygen tank, causing it to explode.

sp-sec10-u2-L3-Q5.0: Only 2 astronauts walked on the Moon during Apollo 11, but 6 successful missions landed 12 astronauts total.
sp-sec10-u2-L3-Q5.1: 6 is the number of successful landings, but each landing had 2 moonwalkers, totaling 12.
sp-sec10-u2-L3-Q5.3: 24 overstates the number; only 12 astronauts walked on the Moon across 6 Apollo missions.

sp-sec10-u2-L3-Q6.1: Apollo 17 in December 1972 was indeed the last crewed Moon mission, with no humans returning since.

sp-sec10-u3-L1-Q1.1: The Space Shuttle was designed with reusable components including the orbiter and solid rocket boosters.

sp-sec10-u3-L1-Q2.0: Ocean splashdowns were used by earlier capsules like Mercury, Gemini, and Apollo, but not the shuttle.
sp-sec10-u3-L1-Q2.1: The shuttle was a glider with wings that landed unpowered on a runway, not by parachute.
sp-sec10-u3-L1-Q2.3: The shuttle had no ability to hover; it was an unpowered glider during its approach and landing.

sp-sec10-u3-L1-Q4.0: The solid rocket boosters were recovered from the ocean and refurbished for reuse.
sp-sec10-u3-L1-Q4.1: The orbiter flew multiple missions and was the shuttle's main reusable component.
sp-sec10-u3-L1-Q4.3: The main engines (RS-25s) were part of the orbiter and were reused with each flight.

sp-sec10-u3-L1-Q6.1: The Space Shuttle program completed 135 missions between 1981 and 2011.

sp-sec10-u3-L2-Q1.1: The shuttle's payload bay was 18 meters long and could carry satellites, station modules, and other large hardware.

sp-sec10-u3-L2-Q2.0: JWST was launched in 2021 on an Ariane 5 rocket, not by the Space Shuttle.
sp-sec10-u3-L2-Q2.2: Chandra was launched by the shuttle in 1999, but Hubble in 1990 is the most famous shuttle-deployed telescope.
sp-sec10-u3-L2-Q2.3: Spitzer was launched on a Delta II rocket in 2003, not by the Space Shuttle.

sp-sec10-u3-L2-Q5.0: Mae Jemison was the first African American woman in space in 1992, not the first American woman.
sp-sec10-u3-L2-Q5.1: Christa McAuliffe was a teacher selected for the shuttle but died in the Challenger disaster before reaching space.
sp-sec10-u3-L2-Q5.3: Peggy Whitson held the record for most time in space by an American woman, but Sally Ride flew first in 1983.

sp-sec10-u3-L2-Q6.0: Two crew members is far too few; the shuttle required at minimum a commander and pilot plus mission specialists.
sp-sec10-u3-L2-Q6.1: Three crew members is below the typical shuttle complement of 5-7 astronauts.
sp-sec10-u3-L2-Q6.3: Up to 12 exceeds the shuttle's capacity; it was designed for a maximum of 7 crew members.

sp-sec10-u3-L3-Q1.1: The Challenger disaster was caused by an O-ring failure in the right solid rocket booster due to cold launch conditions.

sp-sec10-u3-L3-Q2.0: 1981 was the year of the shuttle's first flight; the Challenger disaster occurred in 1986.
sp-sec10-u3-L3-Q2.2: 1996 is a decade too late; the Challenger disaster happened on January 28, 1986.
sp-sec10-u3-L3-Q2.3: 2003 was the year of the Columbia disaster, not the Challenger disaster.

sp-sec10-u3-L3-Q3.0: The engines functioned normally during Columbia's launch; the fatal damage was from foam striking the wing.
sp-sec10-u3-L3-Q3.1: Columbia's computers worked correctly; the problem was physical damage to the thermal protection system.
sp-sec10-u3-L3-Q3.3: The shuttle did not use parachutes for landing; Columbia broke apart during reentry due to heat entering through the damaged wing.

sp-sec10-u3-L3-Q5.0: After both disasters, NASA grounded the shuttle fleet for years while investigating and implementing safety changes.

sp-sec10-u4-L1-Q3.1: Water on the Moon was not confirmed until decades after Explorer 1's 1958 mission.
sp-sec10-u4-L1-Q3.2: The cosmic microwave background was discovered in 1965 by Penzias and Wilson, not by Explorer 1.
sp-sec10-u4-L1-Q3.3: Radio emissions from Jupiter were detected by ground radio telescopes, not by Explorer 1's instruments.

sp-sec10-u4-L1-Q4.0: Gagarin completed exactly one orbit of Earth during his 108-minute Vostok 1 flight.

sp-sec10-u4-L1-Q6.0: Scientific curiosity played a role, but the primary driver was geopolitical competition between the US and USSR.
sp-sec10-u4-L1-Q6.1: The search for alien life was not a motivating factor in the Space Race of the 1950s-60s.
sp-sec10-u4-L1-Q6.3: Commercial satellite business developed later; the Space Race was driven by Cold War rivalry.

sp-sec10-u4-L2-Q1.0: One landing was Apollo 11, but there were 5 more successful landings through Apollo 17.
sp-sec10-u4-L2-Q1.1: Three underestimates; Apollo missions 11, 12, 14, 15, 16, and 17 all successfully landed on the Moon.
sp-sec10-u4-L2-Q1.3: Nine overstates the count; only 6 of the 7 attempted Apollo landings succeeded (Apollo 13 was aborted).

sp-sec10-u4-L2-Q2.0: The Space Shuttle operated only in low Earth orbit and lacked the capability to reach the Moon.

sp-sec10-u4-L2-Q5.0: Challenger was the second shuttle orbiter to fly, not the first.
sp-sec10-u4-L2-Q5.2: Discovery was the third shuttle orbiter to enter service, flying first in 1984.
sp-sec10-u4-L2-Q5.3: Endeavour was built as a replacement for Challenger and first flew in 1992.

sp-sec10-u5-L1-Q1.1: Skylab launched in 1973 and was the first US space station, predating the ISS by decades.

sp-sec10-u5-L1-Q2.0: Skylab was not built from scratch; it was converted from an existing Saturn V S-IVB upper stage.
sp-sec10-u5-L1-Q2.2: The Space Shuttle first flew in 1981, eight years after Skylab launched; it could not have assembled it.
sp-sec10-u5-L1-Q2.3: Skylab was an American station built and launched by the US, not donated by the Soviet Union.

sp-sec10-u5-L1-Q4.0: There was no shuttle available in 1979 to boost Skylab's orbit; it reentered the atmosphere uncontrolled.
sp-sec10-u5-L1-Q4.1: The shuttle did not fly until 1981 and could not have towed Skylab to the ISS, which didn't exist until 1998.
sp-sec10-u5-L1-Q4.3: Skylab's orbit decayed and it reentered Earth's atmosphere in 1979, breaking apart over Australia.

sp-sec10-u5-L1-Q5.1: Skylab's third and final crew stayed aboard for 84 days, setting a US spaceflight duration record at the time.

sp-sec10-u5-L2-Q1.1: Mir was indeed the first space station assembled from multiple modules launched separately and connected in orbit.

sp-sec10-u5-L2-Q2.0: Three years is far too short; Mir operated from 1986 to 2001, a total of 15 years.
sp-sec10-u5-L2-Q2.1: Eight years underestimates Mir's impressive 15-year operational life.
sp-sec10-u5-L2-Q2.3: 25 years overstates Mir's lifespan; it was deorbited in 2001 after 15 years.

sp-sec10-u5-L2-Q4.0: Mir was not preserved in orbit; its orbit decayed and it was intentionally deorbited.
sp-sec10-u5-L2-Q4.1: Boosting Mir higher was not feasible; the aging station was deliberately deorbited for safety.
sp-sec10-u5-L2-Q4.3: The shuttle could not tow Mir; it was far too large, and it was intentionally deorbited into the Pacific Ocean.

sp-sec10-u5-L2-Q5.1: Mir experienced both an onboard fire in 1997 and a collision with a Progress cargo ship, yet continued operating.

sp-sec10-u5-L3-Q1.0: The ISS is a partnership of 15 nations including the US, Russia, Europe, Japan, and Canada.

sp-sec10-u5-L3-Q2.0: 1986 is when Mir launched; ISS assembly began much later with the Zarya module in 1998.
sp-sec10-u5-L3-Q2.1: 1993 is when the ISS partnership was formally agreed, but actual assembly in orbit started in 1998.
sp-sec10-u5-L3-Q2.3: 2005 is too late; the first ISS module launched in 1998 and the station was partially operational by 2000.

sp-sec10-u5-L3-Q4.0: The ISS is far larger than a car; its pressurized volume alone exceeds a six-bedroom house.
sp-sec10-u5-L3-Q4.1: While the pressurized space is house-sized, the full structure with solar panels spans a football field.
sp-sec10-u5-L3-Q4.3: A city block vastly overstates the ISS size; its end-to-end span is about 109 meters.

sp-sec10-u5-L3-Q5.1: The ISS has been continuously inhabited since November 2, 2000, the longest continuous human presence in space.
